jokes.text: Episode coda: Play Now files for bankruptcy because of Claire's lawsuit — George loses his contract pay.
jokes.setup: George staged an oil disaster to revenge himself on Play Now
jokes.punchline: Play Now goes bankrupt from Claire's lawsuit — meaning George's contract pay evaporates
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld structural irony — George's entire season-long scheme to extract money from Play Now is destroyed by the very plan he helped enable (the oil ball). The unintended consequence perfectly punishes George's scheming. Good structural payoff.

jokes.text: 'Whatever happened to Darin?' / 'Darin's going away for a long, long time.'
jokes.setup: Jerry asks about the intern
jokes.punchline: George's flat 'Darin's going away for a long, long time' — implying prison, delivered without ceremony or elaboration
jokes.explanation: A callback payoff to Jerry's earlier prediction about the arrest and trial. 'Darin's going away for a long, long time' as a complete, unexpanded statement is perfect dark comedy economy. The casualness with which George reports a college student's imprisonment is bleakly funny. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Claire sure looked pretty funny all covered in oil.' — then does the belly voice describing it: 'Hello. I got beaned with a giant ball of oil. I'm slippery as an eel.'
jokes.setup: Jerry reflects on the aftermath; Claire was doused in oil
jokes.punchline: He immediately turns Claire's trauma into new belly voice material — doing the voice to describe her oil-covered situation
jokes.explanation: The running gag's final evolution — the belly voice now has new material (the oil incident), and Jerry is delighted. 'I'm slippery as an eel' as a belly voice line is funny and character-appropriate. Good callback closure.

jokes.text: Claire covered in oil doing the voice: 'Hello. I got beaned with a giant ball of oil. I'm slippery as an eel.'
jokes.setup: Claire has been covered in oil from the experiment
jokes.punchline: She's adopted the belly voice herself — and her content is perfectly appropriate: announcing her oily condition in the voice's jovial register
jokes.explanation: Beautiful irony — Claire, who demanded Jerry give up the voice, has now adopted it herself after being hit with oil. 'I got beaned with a giant ball of oil. I'm slippery as an eel.' delivered in the jovial voice is funny content AND a perfect thematic payoff: the voice wins. Resistance was futile.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'I'm just so glad it's back.' — referring to the belly voice, as she and Jerry are now friends again with Puddy gone
jokes.setup: Jerry and Elaine reflect on the belly voice being back in their lives
jokes.punchline: Elaine — who earlier called the voice 'stupid' — is now genuinely glad it's back
jokes.explanation: A satisfying ironic callback — Elaine, who warned against the voice at the start, is now its biggest fan. The character arc is complete and circular in classic Seinfeld fashion.

jokes.text: The final exchange: Puddy declares 'We belong together' — then immediately says 'Elaine, I want to break up.' / Elaine: 'Nuts.'
jokes.setup: The group is gathered; Puddy is cuddling with Elaine and reflecting on how right it all feels
jokes.punchline: Having just said 'we belong together,' Puddy immediately breaks up with Elaine — Elaine's one-word reaction: 'Nuts.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and a perfect Seinfeld ending. The structure is flawless: Puddy sets up the sentiment ('we belong together'), immediately reverses ('I want to break up'), and Elaine's single-word acceptance — 'Nuts' — is simultaneously funny, resigned, and a perfect encapsulation of the show's nihilistic worldview. It's a callback to every bet in the episode, confirms the backslide theory is now running in reverse, and closes the loop with maximum economy. 'Nuts.' might be the single most efficient ending possible. Justifies 9.1: exceptionally original structure (the instantaneous reversal), immaculate construction, could only be Puddy and Elaine, maximally economical, 100% earned by everything that preceded it.

tmdb_overview: Kramer hires an assistant; George's boss tries to force him out; Elaine bets Jerry she won't get back with her latest ex.
insight: George's inner monologue bit carries a mid-tier episode through absurdist escalation alone.
